1: The oracle invokes the Deuteronomic divorce law (Deuteronomy 24:1-4): hēn yĕšallah ʾîš ʾet-ʾišeṭô wĕhālĕkâ mēʾittô wĕhāyĕtâ lĕʾîš-ʾaḥēr hăyāšûḇ ʾēlêhā ʿôd — if a man divorces his wife and she goes and marries another, can he take her back? Deuteronomy 24:4 explicitly forbids this return as defilement of the land. YHWH uses this law as the legal backdrop: Israel (the wife) has given herself to many lovers, making her return legally impossible under Mosaic law — yet the following verses issue the impossible call: return to me. The grace that overrides the law is the theological point.
2: Śĕʾî ʿênayik ʿal-šĕpāyim wĕrĕʾî — raise your eyes to the bare hilltops and look. The šĕpāyim (bare/treeless hilltops) are the locations of Baal shrines. ʾĕ-pō lōʾ šuggalt — is there any place you have not been violated? The passive form (šuggalt, Pual of šāḵaḇ) is used of sexual violence; the violence-language inverts the agency — Israel is depicted as both perpetrator and victim of her own spiritual adultery. Kăʾărāḇî ḇammidbar yāšāḇt lāhem — you sat by the roads like a nomad/ambusher in the desert (waiting for lovers).
3: Wayyimmānĕʿû rĕḇîḇîm — and the latter rain was withheld. The connection is covenant-consequence: the drought is YHWH's disciplinary response (Deuteronomy 28:22-24, withheld rain as covenant curse). Ûmēṣaḥ ʾiššâ zônâ hāyâ lāk — yet you have a prostitute's forehead (mēṣaḥ = forehead, brow). The prostitute's forehead has no shame-response — the physical feature associated with blushing is described as hardened beyond responding. The covenant discipline (drought) has produced no repentance.
4: Hălōʾ mēʿattâ qārāʾt lî ʾāḇî — have you not just now called to me, My Father? The appeal to fatherhood (ʾāḇ) stands alongside the marriage metaphor — YHWH is both husband (Jeremiah 2) and father figure (here). ʾAllûp nĕʿûrāy ʾāttâ — you are the companion of my youth (ʾallûp = intimate companion, confidant); the nostalgic cry invokes the intimacy of the early covenant but is hollow given the conduct of v1-3.
5: Hăyinṭōr lĕʿōlām — will he be angry forever? ʾim-yišmōr lāneṣaḥ — will he keep his anger to the end? These questions are put in Israel's mouth — they reflect a presumption of divine forgiveness without repentance. The answer to the rhetorical questions is implicitly yes — but only because they are backed by behavior: hinnēh dibbartā wĕʿāśît hārāʿôt wattûkāl — behold, you have spoken and done evil, and you prevailed at it (or: you have done all the evil you could do). Words of penitence without changed behavior.
6: The prose section (Jeremiah 3:6-11) uses the two-sisters allegory: mĕšûḇâ Yiśrāʾēl (faithless/apostate Israel) and bōgĕdâ Yĕhûdâ (treacherous/unfaithful Judah). Mĕšûḇâ is from šûḇ — the apostasy-noun; bōgĕdâ is the treachery-participle (from bāgad, to deal treacherously, betray). The two synonymous but distinct terms for the two kingdoms will recur in v7-12.
7: Wĕʾōmar ʾaḥărê ʿăśōtāh ʾēt-kol-ʾēlleh ʾēlay tāšûḇ wĕlōʾ-šāḇâ — I said, after she had done all this, Return to me, but she did not return (šûḇ x2: invitation vs. non-response). Wattereʾ bōgĕdâ ʾăḥôtāh Yĕhûdâ — then her treacherous sister Judah saw it: Judah was the witness of Israel's judgment (the Assyrian conquest, 722 BCE) and should have learned from it.
8: Wĕʾerʾ kî ʿal-kol-ʾōdôt ʾăšer nīʾăpâ mĕšûḇâ Yiśrāʾēl šillahttîhā wĕʾetten ʾēt sēper kĕrîtûtêhā ʾēlêhā — I saw that because faithless Israel had committed adultery I sent her away and gave her a certificate of divorce (sēper kĕrîtûtêhā). The divorce certificate (kĕrîtût, from kārat, to cut off) is the official Mosaic divorce document (Deuteronomy 24:1); YHWH formally divorced the northern kingdom — the Assyrian exile is the enacted divorce. Wĕlōʾ yāreʾâ bōgĕdâ Yĕhûdâ ʾăḥôtāh — yet treacherous Judah her sister had no fear.
9: Wayhî mĕqōl zĕnûtāh wattĕṭammēʾ ʾet-hāʾāreṣ — she defiled the land from the sound/brazenness of her prostitution. Mĕqōl (from the sound/voice) is idiomatically "from the carelessness/brazenness" — the sin was loud and public. Wattinaf ʾet-hāʾeḇen wĕʾet-hāʿēṣ — she committed adultery with stone and tree: the sacred pillars (maṣṣēḇôt) and Asherah poles (ʾăšērîm) of Canaanite worship.
10: Wĕḡam-bĕkol-zōʾt lōʾ-šāḇâ ʾēlay bōgĕdâ Yĕhûdâ ʾăḥôtāh bĕkol-libbāh — in spite of all this, treacherous Judah did not return to me with all her heart. Bĕkol-libbāh (with all her heart) is the Shema's standard (Deuteronomy 6:5); what Judah offered was only bĕšeqer (in pretense/falsehood). Josiah's reform is likely in view — an external reform without deep covenantal transformation of the heart.
11: Tsiddĕqâ napšāh mĕšûḇâ Yiśrāʾēl mibboḡĕdâ Yĕhûdâ — faithless Israel has shown herself more righteous than treacherous Judah. The comparative righteousness is morally paradoxical: the apostate northern kingdom that was divorced is more in the right than the southern kingdom that witnessed the divorce and still pursued the same path. Judah's hypocrisy makes her worse than Israel's open apostasy.
12: Šûḇî mĕšûḇâ Yiśrāʾēl nĕʾum-YHWH — Return, O apostate (mĕšûḇâ) Israel! The paronomasia is the theological heart of Jeremiah 3: the noun mĕšûḇâ (apostasy, literal: turning-away) is from the same root as the imperative šûḇî (return/turn-back); "turn back, O Turn-Away!" The very word that names the problem is transformed into the invitation to its solution. Kî-ḥāsîd ʾănî nĕʾum-YHWH — for I am faithful in covenant love (ḥāsîd, the adjective from ḥesed). YHWH's character is the ground of the invitation — not Israel's deserving.
13: Raq dĕʿî ʿăwōnēk kî bĕYHWH ʾĕlōhayik pāšaʿt — only acknowledge your guilt that you have rebelled against YHWH your God. The conditional is minimal: acknowledgment (dāʿat, knowing = recognition) is the one condition for the invitation of v12 to be activated. The minimal condition forestalls the legalist's objection that return requires works — it requires recognition of reality: pāšaʿt (you have transgressed, Qal perfect — a completed fact that must be owned).
14: Šûḇû bānîm šôbāḇîm nĕʾum-YHWH kî ʾānōkî bāʿalt bākem — Return, faithless children (šôbāḇîm, Poel participle — habitually turning/backsliding), declares YHWH, for I am your husband (bāʿalt bākem). The baʿal (lord/husband/master) language here is charged — the word used for both the Canaanite storm-god and a human husband. YHWH is the true Baʿal (husband) and the Canaanite Baal is the counterfeit. The eschatological promise: wĕlāqaḥtî ʾetkem ʾeḥād mēʿîr ûšnayim mimmišpāḥâ — one from a city and two from a clan — a remnant-gathering, small but certain.
15: Wĕnātattî lākem rōʿîm kĕlibbî — and I will give you shepherds according to my heart (kĕlibbî, lit. like my heart). The construction kĕlibbî is the same as 1 Samuel 13:14 where David is chosen as ʾîš kĕlibbô (a man after his own heart) — the shepherds promised here have the disposition and priorities of YHWH himself. Wĕrāʿû ʾetkem dēʿâ wĕhaśkēl — who will shepherd you with knowledge (dēʿâ) and wisdom (haśkēl): both terms are covenant-epistemological words — the knowing that results in right action and the insight that comes from YHWH-orientation.
16: Lōʾ-yōʾmrû ʿôd ʾărôn bĕrît-YHWH — no one will say any longer, The ark of the covenant of YHWH. This remarkable oracle predicts the obsolescence of the ark — not as loss but as fulfillment. Wĕlōʾ yaʿăleh ʿal-lēḇ — it will not come to mind. The ark was the locus of YHWH's presence (the mercy seat, kappōret), the place where blood was sprinkled on the Day of Atonement, and the symbol of covenant relationship. Its predicted obsolescence anticipates the new covenant's directness of access — what the ark mediated will be directly present. Wĕlōʾ yizkārû wĕlōʾ yippāqēdû wĕlōʾ yēʿāśeh ʿôd — it will not be remembered, not missed, not made again: five negatives emphasizing absolute obsolescence.
17: Baʿēt hahîʾ yiqrĕʾû lîrûšālayim kissēʾ YHWH — at that time they will call Jerusalem the throne of YHWH. The ark was the portable kissēʾ (throne) — YHWH seated between the cherubim; now the entire city becomes the divine throne. The geographic expansion is enormous: from a 2.5-cubit box to an entire city to (Revelation 21:22-23) the entire new creation. Wĕniqqĕwû ʾēlêhā kol-haggôyim — all nations will gather to it: the prophetic motif of Gentile pilgrimage to Jerusalem (Isaiah 2:2-3; Jeremiah 60:1-7; Zechariah 8:20-22) is here connected to the obsolescence of the ark and its replacement by a universal throne.
18: Bayyāmîm hāhēmmâ yēlĕkû bêt-Yĕhûdâ ʿal bêt-Yiśrāʾēl — in those days the house of Judah will join the house of Israel. The eschatological reunification of the divided kingdom (split since 931 BCE) is anticipated; both kingdoms will return together from the north land (mēʾereṣ ṣāpôn) — the Babylonian-Assyrian direction — to the land of the ancestors. The reunion oracle anticipates Ezekiel 37:15-28 (the two sticks joined).
19: Wĕʾānōkî ʾāmartî ʾêk ʾāšîtĕkā ḇabbānîm — I said, How I would set you among the children. The divine monologue of paternal longing: wĕʾettan lĕkā ʾereṣ ḥemdâ naḥălat ṣĕḇî ṣiḇʾôt gôyim — and give you a desirable land, the most beautiful inheritance among the nations. Naḥălat ṣĕḇî (inheritance of beauty) and ṣiḇʾôt gôyim (the beauty/glory of nations) are poetic superlatives for the land of Canaan. Wāʾōmar ʾāḇî tiqrĕʾûlî — I said/expected: you will call me Father. The expected filial response that did not materialize.
20: ʾĀkēn bāgĕdâ ʾiššâ mērēʿāh — but like a woman who is faithless to her husband, the noun rēaʿ (companion, husband) is the covenant-partner term; treachery against a covenant companion (bāgad) is the precise word for covenant betrayal. The sentence shifts back to the marriage metaphor from the paternal metaphor of v19: bāgĕdâ bî bêt Yiśrāʾēl — you have dealt treacherously with me, O house of Israel. The final divine declaration maintains the formal legal language of the lawsuit even in the language of wounded relationship.
21: Qôl ʿal-šĕpāyim nišmaʿ bĕkî taḥănûnê bĕnê Yiśrāʾēl — a voice is heard on the bare hilltops — the weeping and pleading of the children of Israel. The bare hilltops (šĕpāyim) where they worshipped (v2) are now the sites of their lamentation — the oracle moves from indictment to anticipation of repentance. Kî hiwwû ʾet-darkām šāḵĕḥû ʾet-YHWH ʾĕlōhêhem — because they have perverted their ways and forgotten YHWH their God: the two charges (perversion of conduct + forgetting of YHWH) summarize the lawsuit of Jeremiah 2-3.
22: Šûḇû bānîm šôbāḇîm ʾerpāʾ mĕšûḇōtêkem — Return, faithless children, and I will heal your apostasies. The verb rāpāʾ (heal) applied to mĕšûḇôt (apostasies) is striking: apostasy is diagnosed as a disease requiring healing rather than solely as a crime requiring punishment. The response (in the same verse, the voice of the returning people): hinnēnû bāʾnû ʾēlêkā kî ʾattâ YHWH ʾĕlōhênû — here we are, we come to you, for you are YHWH our God. The return-formula is the direct antithesis of Jeremiah 2:25 (I love other gods and after them I will go).
23: ʾĀkēn laššeqer mēgibʿôt hāmôn hārîm — surely the commotion on the hills and mountains is a delusion/in vain. Laššeqer (for deception, in vain) pronounces the idolatry hollow — the noise of the high-place worship was empty. The confession affirms: ʾak bĕYHWH ʾĕlōhênû tĕšûʿat Yiśrāʾēl — but in YHWH our God is Israel's salvation (tĕšûʿâ). The salvation vocabulary (yāšaʿ root) is used of YHWH alone, not of the hills or mountains or idols.
24: Wĕhabbōšet ʾākĕlâ ʾet-yĕgîaʿ ʾăḇôtênû — the shameful thing has consumed what our fathers worked for. Habbōšet (the shameful thing) is a substitute name for Baal — just as the prophets use bōšet (shame) for Baal-names (cf. Ishbaal/Ishbosheth; Mephibosheth/Meribaal). Everything the ancestors acquired — flocks and herds, sons and daughters — the idol-worship has consumed: the economic, social, and generational capital has been drained by the shame-worship.
25: Niškĕḇâ bĕḇōšāṯēnû ûtĕkassēnû kĕlimmātēnû — let us lie down in our shame and let our disgrace cover us. The communal confession takes the prostration posture (šāḵaḇ, lie down) of defeat and shame — the opposite of the proud standing of the shrine-worshipper. Kî lĕYHWH ʾĕlōhênû ḥāṭāʾnû ʾănaḥnû ûʾăḇôtênû — for we have sinned against YHWH our God, both we and our fathers, from our youth to this day. The confession is corporate, generational, and temporal — it encompasses the entire history of unfaithfulness from covenant inception to the present moment.
